# Trellis AI guidance for TypeScript services

Last verified against Trellis main (2026-06-16).

Use this file for AI coding agents working in a TypeScript service repository
that consumes Trellis. The language-neutral guide is:

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/qlever-llc/trellis/main/docs/static/llms-full.txt

For Svelte browser apps, use:

https://raw.githubusercontent.com/qlever-llc/trellis/main/docs/static/llms-svelte.txt

## Service repository setup

Recommended TypeScript service shape:

```text
services/<name>/
|-- deno.json or package.json
|-- contract.ts              # Single-contract public entrypoint
|-- contracts/*.ts           # Optional multi-contract modules
|-- schemas/*.ts             # TypeBox wire schemas and exported model schemas
|-- errors/*.ts              # Service-local transportable errors
|-- rpc/*.ts                 # RPC handlers
|-- events/*.ts              # Event listeners and publishers
|-- operations/*.ts          # Operation providers
|-- db/ and migrations/      # Service-local storage and migrations
|-- config.ts                # Zod environment/config parsing
`-- main.ts                  # Bootstrap, registration, shutdown
```

Rules:

- single-contract services normally expose `contract.ts`; multi-contract services
  may use `contracts/*.ts` and optionally re-export the primary default contract
  from `contract.ts`
- every TypeScript contract source loaded by Trellis generation should
  default-export the `defineServiceContract(...)`, `defineAppContract(...)`,
  `defineAgentContract(...)`, or `defineDeviceContract(...)` result
- import those normal contract helpers from the browser-safe
  `@qlever-llc/trellis` root. Keep service runtime, device activation,
  browser-auth, telemetry, generated SDK, and advanced contract tooling imports on
  their explicit subpaths.
- `deno.json` should expose Trellis generation through `deno task prepare`
- service-boundary integration tests can use `@qlever-llc/trellis-test`:
  start it with an explicit `trellis.command` for the control-plane process, then
  call `runtime.registerService(...)`, service `TrellisService.connect(...)`, and
  `runtime.connectClient(...)` for app/client participants. Use
  `runtime.captureEvents(...)` with `assertEventCaptured`,
  `assertEventsCaptured`, `assertNoEventCaptured`, or `assertNoEventDuring` for
  live generated event assertions. Use `assertJobCompleted`,
  `assertOperationCompleted`, `assertRpcOk`, `assertRpcEventuallyOk`, and
  `assertRpcErr` for terminal job, operation, immediate Result-style RPC, and
  eventual projection assertions. Generated event captures and generated service
  job refs can be passed directly to those helpers without local adapters. These
  helpers exercise real bootstrap, auth, authority, resources, events, jobs, and
  operations through generated client surfaces
- the Trellis repo's language-owned JS client integration suite also uses
  `@qlever-llc/trellis-test` and runs with
  `deno task -c js/deno.json test:integration`. Focused repo-local runs support
  `--fixture`, `--case`, and `--coverage` filters, all backed by
  `integration/client-test-matrix.json`; every matrix case must also be
  registered by the Rust `trellis-rs` integration suite.
- the `prepare` task value should call the Trellis generator, for example
  `deno run -A @qlever-llc/trellis/generate prepare .`
- generated SDKs, manifests, and package outputs belong under the service repo's
  generated output locations; do not edit generated files by hand
- after changing a contract, run `deno task prepare` before typing extracted
  handlers so generated SDK aliases match the current surfaces
- Trellis caches manifests by digest at runtime. If auth/bootstrap reports a
  missing digest after the cache is pruned, regenerate from source and present the
  full manifest; do not patch deployment authority or implementation offers by
  hand.
- Deployment authority proposal/desired `needs` are grouped by `contracts`,
  `surfaces`, `capabilities`, and `resources`. Materialized authority is a
  projection with runtime `grants` grouped by `capabilities`, `surfaces`, and
  `nats`; service permissions require current materialization for the accepted
  desired authority version. Stale or obsolete persisted projections are repaired
  by Trellis storage upgrade and reconciliation.

## Use surface-first APIs

Use generated facades on the connected client:

```ts
const order = await client.rpc.orders.get({ orderId: "ord_123" }).orThrow();

await client.event.orders.shipped.publish({
  orderId: "ord_123",
  customerId: "cust_456",
  shippedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
}).orThrow();

const feed = await client.feed.orders.live({ customerId: "cust_456" }).orThrow();

const op = await client.operation.orders.process
  .start({ orderId: "ord_123" })
  .orThrow();
```

If a generated facade is missing, check the contract generation path or the
current API reference.

For cross-contract calls, declare a narrow `uses` selection and call the other
contract SDK's `use(spec)` helper with only the blocks needed by this service.
Avoid broad selections just to make generated client types appear.

Connected TypeScript clients, devices, and services do not expose raw NATS
handles. Use generated `rpc`, `event`, `feed`, `operation`, `state`, and
`transfer` surfaces for communication, `service.kv` / `service.store` /
`service.jobs` for service-owned resources, and `connection` for lifecycle status
or shutdown.

For browser app sign-out, use Trellis browser auth helpers so the app signs and
POSTs the logout request over HTTP before navigating to any returned redirect.
Do not call `Auth.Sessions.Logout` from the active app connection for normal
browser logout, and let Trellis validate `returnTo` and build provider logout
URLs instead of hard-coding IdP endpoints.

For integration tests, prefer `@qlever-llc/trellis-test` over raw NATS subjects
or hand-built bootstrap responses. The test runtime requires an explicit
`trellis.command`, creates an isolated NATS/JetStream environment, and starts the
real Trellis control plane as a spawned process; services under test still
connect through public `TrellisService.connect(...)`. Use
`runtime.connectClient(...)` or `runtime.registerClient(...)` plus
`runtime.clientAuth(...)` for app/client participants, use
`runtime.captureEvents(...)` for live event assertions with decoded
`{ event, payload, context, receivedAt }` records, and prefer the exported
assertion helpers for captured events, terminal jobs and operations, and
immediate or eventual `Result` RPC values. Generated captures and job refs are
accepted directly. Allow migration plans only in explicit isolated mutable-dev
tests with `authority.autoAccept` or per-approval `allowPlanClassifications`.

For service-private jobs that must be singleton per logical key, declare
`keyConcurrency` on the job queue. Keep the existing numeric `concurrency`
field for worker-count concurrency. Keyed queues default to clear failure
(`stalePolicy: "fail-stale"`, `whenFull: "reject"`); only use
`whenFull: "coalesce"` or `"replace-oldest"` when the service explicitly wants
scheduler-style compression or supersession. `create(...)` means “create a new
job or return a typed failure”; use the additive `submit(...)` helper when code
needs to inspect accepted/rejected/coalesced/replaced outcomes.

## Register handlers with `service.handle`

Register providers through the generated `service.handle` tree. Handler code
receives typed input and a scoped `client` for allowed outbound calls. Trellis
does not provide application dependency injection; handler arguments contain only
Trellis-owned runtime data. Application resources are supplied by normal
JavaScript closure or factory patterns.

```ts
import { Result } from "@qlever-llc/trellis";

await service.handle.rpc.orders.get(async ({ input, client }) => {
  const row = await loadOrder(input.orderId);
  if (!row) return Result.err(orderNotFound(input.orderId));

  await client.event.orders.viewed.publish({
    orderId: input.orderId,
    viewedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
  }).orThrow();

  return Result.ok(row);
});
```

The scoped `client` represents the service's allowed outbound dependencies.
Register event listeners during service startup with `service.event`.

When handlers need application-owned dependencies, capture them in a closure:

```ts
import { Result } from "@qlever-llc/trellis";
import type { OrdersGetHandler } from "@my-org/orders-service-sdk";

const deps = { db, logger };

await service.handle.rpc.orders.get(async ({ input, client }) => {
  const row = await deps.db.orders.get(input.orderId);
  if (!row) return Result.err(orderNotFound(input.orderId));

  deps.logger.info({ orderId: input.orderId }, "loaded order");
  await client.event.orders.viewed.publish({ orderId: input.orderId }).orThrow();

  return Result.ok(row);
});

export const getOrder: OrdersGetHandler = async ({ input, client }) => {
  const row = await deps.db.orders.get(input.orderId);
  if (!row) return Result.err(orderNotFound(input.orderId));

  await client.event.orders.viewed.publish({ orderId: input.orderId }).orThrow();
  return Result.ok(row);
};
```

Inline handlers infer from the `service.handle...` registration site. Extracted
RPC, event, feed, operation, and job handlers should use concrete handler aliases
from the generated SDK, for example `OrdersGetHandler`.

## Schemas and errors

- Use TypeBox for contract wire schemas: RPC input/output, event payloads, feed
  input/events, operation input/output/progress, and resource declarations.
- Event subject `params` are JSON Pointers into the event payload. They must
  resolve to string, number, or integer-compatible schema nodes. For `anyOf` or
  `oneOf` event payload schemas, every branch must contain each routed pointer.
- Use Zod for environment and local configuration parsing.
- Use Trellis pagination helpers for list RPCs instead of inventing new page
  envelopes. Offset pagination uses `PageRequestSchema`,
  `PageResponseSchema(entry)`, `normalizePageQuery(...)`, and
  `buildPageResponse(...)`. Stable ID/keyset pagination uses `CursorQuerySchema`,
  `CursorPageSchema(item)`, `CursorPageResponseSchema<TItem>`,
  `normalizeCursorQuery(...)`, and `buildCursorPage(...)`.
- Use `withTrellisValidation(schema, extension)` from
  `@qlever-llc/trellis/contracts` to attach UI-facing `x-trellis-validation`
  metadata to TypeBox schema nodes. The metadata is purely for UX and does not
  change validation behavior.
- When every pre-handler schema validation failure is annotated, Trellis returns
  `SchemaValidationError` instead of `ValidationError` — the handler does not
  run in either case. `SchemaValidationError` carries an `issues[]` array with
  stable field-level UX information (path, keyword, code, message, label, note,
  severity, params). A single structural or unannotated failure downgrades the
  entire response to `ValidationError`.
- Return declared business errors through `Result` values. Use `.orThrow()` only
  at process boundaries where throwing is intentional.
- Extracted handler aliases accept source `defineError(...)` instances when their
  serialized data matches the generated declared error data. Client-side SDKs
  still reconstruct declared errors as generated SDK classes.

## Operation Typed Errors
- Operations support declared expected errors: `errors: [ref.error(...)]`
- Declared operation errors narrow `op.fail(error)` to the declared error union plus Trellis runtime errors
- Generated SDK types include `{Base}OperationHandlerError` aliases
- Manifest: operation descriptors carry `errors: [{ type: "..." }]`

## Prepared events and outbox/inbox

Direct publish is the default when no local transaction must be coupled to the
event:

```ts
await client.event.orders.shipped.publish({
  orderId: input.orderId,
  customerId: input.customerId,
  shippedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
}).orThrow();
```

Generated SDK event types are event bodies. Trellis event metadata is assigned by
the runtime and is available as `PreparedTrellisEvent.header` after `prepare(...)`
and as `context.id` / `context.time` in event listeners.

For integration tests that must observe a published live event, start
`runtime.captureEvents(...)` before publishing through a generated event facade.
Assert the capture record's decoded `payload` and metadata instead of subscribing
to raw NATS subjects or relying only on outbox rows.

Use `service.createSqlOutbox(...)` when a service-local SQL transaction and event
enqueue must commit or roll back together. Configure it once during startup with
a generic SQL `executor` for dispatcher work and a `transaction` runner that
provides the service transaction object plus a transaction-scoped Trellis
`SqlExecutor`. The outbox object is a plain dependency that handlers close over.

```ts
import {
  type SqlExecutor,
  type TrellisServiceSqlOutboxOptions,
} from "@qlever-llc/trellis/service";
import { Result } from "@qlever-llc/trellis";

const sqlOutbox: TrellisServiceSqlOutboxOptions<AppTransaction> = {
  dialect: "postgres",
  executor: poolExecutor,
  transaction: async (work) =>
    pool.transaction(async (tx) =>
      work({ tx, executor: createTransactionExecutor(tx) })
    ),
};

function createTransactionExecutor(tx: AppTransaction): SqlExecutor {
  return {
    query: (statement, params) => tx.query(statement, params),
    execute: async (statement, params) => {
      await tx.execute(statement, params);
    },
  };
}

const outbox = service.createSqlOutbox(sqlOutbox);
const deps = { ordersRepo, auditRepo, outbox };

await service.handle.rpc.orders.ship(async ({ input }) => {
  await deps.outbox.transaction(async ({ tx, event }) => {
    await deps.ordersRepo.markShipped(tx, input.orderId);
    await deps.auditRepo.insert(tx, { entityId: input.orderId });

    await event.orders.shipped.enqueue({
      orderId: input.orderId,
      customerId: input.customerId,
      shippedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
    }).orThrow();
    await event.audit.recorded.enqueue({
      entityId: input.orderId,
      action: "order.shipped",
    }).orThrow();
  }).orThrow();

  return Result.ok({ accepted: true });
});
```

Rules for agents:

- close over the outbox object as a plain dependency when handlers need
  transactional enqueue; handlers do not receive `outbox` as an injected argument
- wrap all service DB writes and all event enqueues that must be atomic in
  `outbox.transaction(...)`
- use transaction-scoped `event.*.*.enqueue(...)` only inside that callback;
  normal event facades expose `prepare`, `publish`, and `listen`, not `enqueue`
- multiple DB writes and multiple event enqueues in one transaction are supported
- do not generate ad hoc local outbox wrappers, manual `SqlOutboxRepository` /
  `OutboxDispatcher` handler wiring, or per-handler Drizzle transaction wrappers

Trellis owns SQL helper-table migration artifacts. Services choose table names
and run the artifacts through their normal database migration tool:

```ts
import { getSqlOutboxMigrations } from "@qlever-llc/trellis/service";

const migrations = getSqlOutboxMigrations({
  dialect: "postgres",
  tables: { outbox: "trellis_outbox", inbox: "trellis_inbox" },
});

for (const migration of migrations) {
  await migrationRunner.register({
    id: migration.id,
    checksum: migration.checksum,
    up: migration.up,
    down: migration.down ?? [],
  });
}
```

Drizzle remains optional through `@qlever-llc/trellis/service/drizzle` adapter
helpers. There is intentionally no direct `createSqlOutbox({ drizzle })` sugar.
If a service uses Drizzle, adapt it into the generic `executor` and `transaction`
options at startup; treat `createDrizzleSqlExecutor(...)` as advanced/adapter
usage, not a normal per-handler pattern.

Use `SqlInboxRepository` only when replaying a message would repeat a
non-idempotent side effect. Register the listener during service startup, not
inside a handler.

Use `NatsKvOutboxRepository` and `NatsKvInboxRepository` for services that do not
have SQL state. They are durable helpers, but they are not atomic with unrelated
database transactions.

## Durable event consumers

Direct `uses.events.subscribe` grants only live or ephemeral listening. A service
that needs replay, ordered processing, or a durable cursor must declare a
top-level `eventConsumers` group in its contract and register the startup
listener with that group. `eventConsumers.<group>.uses` selects dependency events
by top-level `uses.required` or `uses.optional` alias;
`eventConsumers.<group>.self` selects events owned by the same contract.

```ts
const contract = defineServiceContract({ schemas }, () => ({
  id: "shipment-projection@v1",
  displayName: "Shipment Projection",
  description: "Projects shipment events into local state.",
  uses: {
    required: {
      orders: orders.use({
        events: { subscribe: ["Orders.Shipped"] },
      }),
    },
  },
  eventConsumers: {
    shipmentProjection: {
      uses: {
        orders: ["Orders.Shipped"],
      },
      replay: "new",
      ordering: "strict",
      concurrency: 1,
    },
  },
}));

await service.event.orders.shipped.listen(handleShipment, {}, {
  group: "shipmentProjection",
}).orThrow();
```

Dependency durable consumption remains authority-backed by the top-level `uses`
subscription. For durable self-consumption of events owned by the same service,
use `self: ["Entity.Observation"]`. Runtime durable consumers are
Trellis-provisioned only; service code should not create or name arbitrary
JetStream durable consumers.

## Verification

Use the format, typecheck, lint, test, run, and migration commands configured by
the service repository. If contract artifacts or generated SDKs changed, run
`deno task prepare` and commit the generated outputs expected by that repo.
